title: Painful Moments
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-26-23 -AM- Painful Moments - Psalm 73 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Faith Crises Are Common
subpoints: The Bible speaks honestly about the frailty of men and the real struggles of the Christian life
Asaph: a spiritual leader, one of three musicians appointed by David to oversee temple worship
The fact that a spiritual leader struggled matters — we must not put pastors on pedestals
Asaph starts with the conclusion: "Truly God is good to Israel" — he is okay now; let me tell you what happened

outline.title: The Crisis: Envy of the Wicked (Psalm 73:2-16)
subpoints: Asaph almost denied his faith — his feet had almost stumbled
He was envious of the boastful and saw the prosperity of the wicked
The wicked had no pains, no trouble, no plague — their eyes bulge with abundance
They mock heaven, set their mouths against God, persecute the people of God
Asaph: "Surely I have cleansed my heart in vain and washed my hands in innocence"
His crisis: not questioning if God exists but being upset with the God he knows exists
Searching for a reason for faith "under the sun" is a futile activity (Ecclesiastes)
When we serve the Lord as a means to get what we want, we may end up where Asaph was

outline.title: The Turn: Going to the Sanctuary (Psalm 73:17)
subpoints: "Until I went into the sanctuary of God — then I understood their end"
Real faith brings its doubts to God; false faith walks away with its doubts
Asaph is not claiming a direct revelation — only correct thinking according to the order of God
The ordinary means of grace help us see all of life from the right perspective
Fellowship, preaching of the Word, prayer, Lord's Supper — available to every Christian

outline.title: The Resolution: Right Thinking About the Wicked (Psalm 73:18-22)
subpoints: Asaph now sees the wicked for what they are: set in slippery places, brought to desolation in a moment
He repents of his foolishness: "I was so foolish and ignorant — I was like a beast before you"
Sin is always irrational — there are no good justifications for walking away from God

outline.title: The Conclusion: God's Goodness Confirmed (Psalm 73:23-28)
subpoints: Even through the crisis, God was holding onto Asaph by his right hand
God will hold him all the way to glory (Philippians 1:6)
"Whom have I in heaven but You? And there is none upon earth that I desire beside You"
God is his "portion" — his entire inheritance, his all
"It is good for me to draw near to God" — the practical conclusion
Right thinking precedes right feeling — not the other way around

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Psalm 73, taking one more Sunday away from the 1 Peter series to address the theme of faith crises. Asaph was a spiritual leader in Israel — one of three musicians appointed by David to oversee temple worship — yet he struggled with a profound crisis of faith when he saw the apparent prosperity of the wicked and the suffering of the righteous. The pastor uses Asaph's example to address several important truths: that spiritual leaders are not immune to doubts and struggles; that faith crises are a common feature of the Christian experience; that searching for reasons to believe God by looking at the ungodly is a futile endeavor; and that the turn in Asaph's crisis came when he went into the sanctuary of God, where through the ordinary means of grace he was enabled to think rightly about life. The resolution is threefold: Asaph sees the true end of the wicked (slippery places, desolation), repents of his foolishness (sin is always irrational), and rediscovers that God is his strength, his guide, and his portion forever. The sermon closes with a direct pastoral encouragement: do not waste your crisis of faith — bring your doubts, complaints, and struggles to God through the ordinary means He has provided, and your faith will be stronger for it.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 73

If you're able to remain standing for one more minute, we're turning to Psalm 73. Psalm 73 is a little longer reading than we're used to, but I think we can stand the reading of God's Word.

"Truly God is good to Israel, to such as are pure in heart. But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled; my steps had nearly slipped. For I was envious of the boastful when I saw the prosperity of the wicked. For there are no pains in their death, but their strength is firm. They are not in trouble as other men, nor are they plagued like other men. Therefore pride serves as their necklace; violence covers them like a garment. Their eyes bulge with abundance; they have more than heart could wish. They scoff and speak wickedly concerning oppression; they speak loftily. They set their mouth against the heavens, and their tongue walks through the earth. Therefore his people return here, and waters of a full cup are drained by them. And they say, 'How does God know? And is there knowledge in the Most High?' Behold, these are the ungodly, who are always at ease; they increase in riches. Surely I have cleansed my heart in vain and washed my hands in innocence. For all day long I have been plagued and chastened every morning. If I had said, 'I will speak thus,' behold, I would have been untrue to the generation of your children. When I thought how to understand this, it was too painful for me — until I went into the sanctuary of God; then I understood their end. Surely You set them in slippery places; You cast them down to destruction. Oh, how they are brought to desolation, as in a moment! They are utterly consumed with terrors. As a dream when one awakes, so, Lord, when You awake, You shall despise their image. Thus my heart was grieved, and I was vexed in my mind. I was so foolish and ignorant; I was like a beast before You. Nevertheless I am continually with You; You hold me by my right hand. You will guide me with Your counsel, and afterward receive me to glory. Whom have I in heaven but You? And there is none upon earth that I desire beside You. My flesh and my heart fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever. For indeed, those who are far from You shall perish; You have destroyed all those who desert You for harlotry. But it is good for me to draw near to God; I have put my trust in the Lord GOD, that I may declare all Your works."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, heavenly Father, pray that you open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning you from this Psalm. We pray that the preaching of your word will be attended by your Spirit and that you write these words deeply in the tablets of our hearts. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: Faith Crises Are Common

Life is filled with painful moments. And faith crises are common in the life of a Christian. We like to think that none of us struggle with our faith. We like to think that we are so strong that there is never a doubt, never a wavering. But that is not true. A struggle of faith is a common feature in the life of the Christian.

I am always so amazed by how the Bible is truly a book for all seasons. The Bible speaks about the frailty of men. It speaks about how the life of a Christian is in real terms — not through rose-colored glasses, not as a bed of roses, but as life really is — a life lived in a broken world full of struggles. The Bible doesn't paint a picture that isn't true. It speaks about every experience and every element of the life of the Christian. So the Apostle Peter is not using hyperbolic language when he says that God has given us all things that pertain to life and godliness.

In this Psalm, Asaph faces a crisis of faith. He is envious of what the unbeliever has. He can't see the point of serving God. He is ready to walk away from faith in God and from the community of God's people — until he thinks correctly. That's what we're going to try to see here this morning.

## Who Is Asaph?

If you look at Psalm 73, you will find the title: "A Psalm of Asaph." I believe the titles are part of the Psalms — in the manuscripts we have in Hebrew, the title of the Psalm is actually verse 1 in the Psalm. So we can be certain this was written by a man named Asaph.

Asaph was one of three musicians appointed by King David to oversee Tabernacle and later Temple worship — and not only that, but to produce songs to be sung on behalf of the people of God. Old Testament worship was vicarious: the common people worshiped God through the priests and Levites, and the Levites would sing these songs on their behalf. Asaph was a godly man, a spiritual leader of the church in the old covenant. And we find him here struggling with his faith.

This fact is important for us to consider. We often put spiritual leaders on pedestals and think that somehow they are not susceptible to the struggles of faith that common people go through. It is true that the sins of pastors, as we learned this morning, are more heinous than the sins of others in the church. At the same time, we must not hold pastors in a position where they are thought of as incapable of sin. That is a pressure pastors should not have to carry.

When we cultivate the idea that spiritual leaders don't struggle, we set up two unintended results. First, we don't allow spiritual leaders to express their struggles and find help. By the time a leader admits something is wrong, it may be too late. Second, there is greater hurt in the church when a spiritual leader does struggle — because it is unexpected.

And yet here Asaph, a spiritual leader in the old covenant church, is very honest about how he struggled. He released this Psalm for the church to sing — he gave us words to sing about our own struggles before the LORD. The biggest thing Asaph is teaching us is that struggles of faith are part of the Christian experience.

## He Starts with the Conclusion

Notice that Asaph starts with the conclusion in verse 1: "Truly God is good to Israel, to such as are pure in heart."

It is as if Asaph is saying to his readers: I'm okay now. I've worked through this issue. The LORD has helped me. But I'm going to tell you some serious struggles I had, and then show you how the LORD brought me through them. That's how Asaph starts — everything's okay now; let me tell you what happened.

We will come back to this truth that God is good, but it is important — so important — for the Christian to realize that God is good to His people, even in the middle of a faith crisis.

## The Crisis: Envy of the Wicked (Psalm 73:2-16)

Look at verses 2-3: "But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled; my steps had nearly slipped. For I was envious of the boastful when I saw the prosperity of the wicked."

Asaph's faith was almost broken because he was envious of the proud — he saw that God was apparently prospering those who did not obey Him. He looked around and saw the wicked having the things that Asaph himself wanted. This is true Hebrew parallelism: "the boastful" in the first line is the same as "the wicked" in the second line.

Notice that often a crisis of faith is not questioning whether God exists, but getting upset with the God we know exists. Asaph is not denying that God exists in his crisis — he is just upset with the God he knows to exist. I was thinking this week of Abraham Piper, the oldest son of Pastor John Piper. He is now a self-proclaimed atheist. And yet when you read his writings, you see great hatred for this God he says doesn't exist — as if he's saying: God doesn't exist, and I hate Him. Which is not exactly a coherent position.

Verses 4-5: "For there are no pains in their death, but their strength is firm. They are not in trouble as other men, nor are they plagued like other men."

Asaph doesn't see any consequences for the wickedness of the ungodly. They are proud of their wickedness (v. 6). They have more than heart could wish (v. 7). They mock heaven and speak against God (vv. 8-9). And they mock and persecute God's people (v. 10-11).

So in verses 12-14, Asaph says: "Behold, these are the ungodly, who are always at ease; they increase in riches. Surely I have cleansed my heart in vain and washed my hands in innocence. For all day long I have been plagued and chastened every morning."

Have you ever been there? Serving the LORD with all your heart, but all you see in your life is suffering, while the wicked prosper without consequence?

In verses 15-16: "If I had said 'I will speak thus,' behold, I would have been untrue to the generation of your children. When I thought how to understand this, it was too painful for me."

Asaph was going through a very real crisis of faith. He was looking for a reason for faith "under the sun" — looking at the world and its ungodly inhabitants and trying to find God there. This is what Solomon tells us in Ecclesiastes is a futile activity. We cannot find a reason for our faith in this world alone. We have to transcend this world in order to find a reason to continue believing.

Brothers and sisters, what happened to Asaph happens to us when we serve the LORD because we think He owes us what we want. God becomes a means to an end rather than the end itself. And when we look at the wicked getting the things we thought we should receive for our obedience, we end up in the same place as Asaph.

This also happens to us when we try to understand God by looking solely at what is going on in the lives of the wicked. We look at a broken world and we will see only brokenness. We need to transcend this world to see clearly.

Before 1960, about 98% of the hymns in hymnals were about heaven and the life to come. After the baby boomer generation took leadership in the church, that completely reversed — fewer than 2% are about heaven. Everything became about improving this life. Which is not wrong in itself — but when this life becomes ultimate, we are going to struggle with faith. Because the wicked do prosper in this life. But that is not the whole story.

## The Turn: The Sanctuary of God (Psalm 73:17)

"Until I went into the sanctuary of God — then I understood their end."

Asaph had real faith. We know this from verse 1, where he identifies himself with the pure in heart — those whose hearts have been changed by the Spirit of God. And because his faith is real, he brings his doubts, his struggles, and his complaints to God Himself instead of walking away from the LORD.

Real faith brings its doubts to God. False faith walks away from the LORD with its doubts. So he brings his doubts to the only One who can actually answer them.

He went into the sanctuary of God. In Asaph's context, the old covenant, this is the Temple — the place where God meets with His people, where His presence is. But it represents the place where God meets us: the corporate worship of God's people, fellowship with Him through the indwelling Spirit, prayer, reading and meditating on God's Word.

Notice that when Asaph says "then I understood their end," he is not claiming a direct message from the LORD — a new, private revelation. He is simply claiming proper thinking according to the order of God, according to who God is. He is simply able to interpret life from the right perspective.

Brothers and sisters, the ordinary means of grace help us see all of life from the right perspective. We often want only the extraordinary — the direct message, the dramatic experience. And yet we have a God who works through the ordinary: the ordinary reading and preaching of the Word, the ordinary prayer, the ordinary partaking of the Lord's Supper, the ordinary fellowship with the saints. These are available to every Christian. And they are what bring us out of a struggle of faith and into walking with the LORD.

## The Resolution: Right Thinking (Psalm 73:18-26)

In verses 18-20, Asaph's perspective has been completely transformed: "Surely You set them in slippery places; You cast them down to destruction. Oh, how they are brought to desolation, as in a moment! They are utterly consumed with terrors. As a dream when one awakes, so, Lord, when You awake, You shall despise their image."

Now he realizes what the wicked truly are and what is coming to them. The life of the wicked is not as glamorous as it seems. Existence apart from God is empty — the scripture calls it a vapor that is here today and gone tomorrow. The wicked continuing in wickedness will be separated from God forever.

Bringing his doubts before the LORD brought Asaph to repentance. In verses 21-22: "Thus my heart was grieved, and I was vexed in my mind. I was so foolish and ignorant; I was like a beast before You."

He now says the same thing about his sins as God would say. He realizes how foolish it was to think that following the wicked would be better than following God. He recognizes it was irrational: "I was like a beast before You" — something that doesn't think.

Brothers and sisters, sin is always irrational. That is what makes sin sin. Walking away from God is always irrational. We are good at making justifications in our heads — but sin is always irrational.

In the resolution, Asaph realizes at least three things (vv. 23-24):

First: "Nevertheless, I am continually with You; You hold me by my right hand." Even through his crisis of faith, God was holding onto him and would not let him go. Paul echoes this in Philippians 1:6: "Being confident of this very thing, that He who has begun a good work in you will complete it until the day of Jesus Christ."

Second: "You will guide me with Your counsel, and afterward receive me to glory." God will hold onto him all the way to glory. The wicked perish, but he will be the LORD's all the way to glory.

Third: He realizes that neither of these things is true of the wicked — so he should not have been envious of them.

And then in verses 25-26, God in His mercy and goodness helps Asaph find his satisfaction in God rather than in the wicked:

"Whom have I in heaven but You? And there is none upon earth that I desire beside You. My flesh and my heart fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever."

He is not saying he has no other desires. He is saying he doesn't desire anything else the way he desires God. That desire is reserved for God and God alone. And he says this is objectively true even when his flesh and heart fail — even when his whole person fails. God is still his strength and his portion. "Portion" in the Bible means his all — his entire inheritance assigned to him, everything that is his. That is what Asaph says God is to him.

## Conclusion: Don't Waste Your Crisis

In verse 28: "But it is good for me to draw near to God; I have put my trust in the Lord GOD, that I may declare all Your works."

Asaph is now thinking straight. His anguish, his jealousy, his despair, his anger are gone — because he has been able to think about life properly. Right feeling starts with right thinking. Asaph understands again what happens to the wicked. He understands what is good for him. His trust in God is renewed and he is ready to declare the works of God.

I'm certain there are many struggling with their faith in our midst. We are very good at putting masks on so that people don't see what's going on in our hearts. If you are struggling with your faith, learn from Asaph. Instead of running away from God, run to God. Bring your doubts, bring your complaints, bring your struggles to Him. Come before His presence through the ordinary means He has given you — available to all of you — so you might understand what God is. Don't look for God in the lives of the wicked. Transcend this world so you can find Him.

Brothers and sisters, don't waste your crisis of faith. Don't waste your crisis of faith. It's painful — so put it to good use. Take your doubts, your complaints, your struggles to the LORD. Use the ordinary means God has given us for very life. He will help you understand life from the perspective of heaven, and your faith will be better for it.

Let us pray together. Heavenly Father, we thank you for your word. We thank you that you address every area of life, every area of thinking, every corner of our hearts. Father, we often believe, but there are areas of unbelief in our lives. So we pray that you would help our unbelief. Father, pray that we would bring our doubts, our struggles, our complaints to you — that we might understand what's going on. Father, pray that indeed you would be our treasure in heaven, that nothing on earth will compare to that. Be present with us even in our struggles. Help us to see that you're going to take us to glory while the wicked will perish. In Jesus' name, amen.
